slides.title: What Lives Inside a for Loop?
slides.body: Every time you write `for x in something`, Python is secretly using an **iterator** behind the scenes. Understanding this unlocks a whole new level of Python.
slides.tip: An iterable is the playlist. An iterator is the play button — it tracks where you are.

slides.title: iter() and next() — The Core Tools
slides.body: Use `iter()` to get an iterator from any iterable, then call `next()` to pull out items one at a time. When items run out, you get `StopIteration`.
slides.code:
numbers = [10, 20, 30]
it = iter(numbers)
print(next(it))
print(next(it, "no more"))
slides.tip: Pass a default to next() as a second argument to avoid a crash when items run out.

slides.title: The Secret Life of for Loops
slides.body: A `for` loop is just `iter()` + repeated `next()` + silently catching `StopIteration`. Python handles all of that for you.
slides.tip: StopIteration is a signal, not an error — for loops catch it quietly and exit.

slides.title: Build Your Own Iterator
slides.body: Give any class `__iter__` and `__next__` methods and Python's `for` loop — plus `list()`, `sum()`, and more — will work with it automatically.
slides.tip: Iterators are one-shot — once exhausted, looping again produces nothing. Create a new instance to restart.

slides.title: yield: The Easy Shortcut
slides.body: A **generator function** using `yield` creates a full iterator automatically — no class needed. It also computes values **one at a time**, so it barely uses memory.
slides.tip: yield pauses the function and resumes exactly where it left off on the next next() call.

slides.title: You Now Get Iterators!
slides.body: You know what really happens inside `for` loops, how to use `iter()` and `next()` directly, how to build custom iterator classes, and how `yield` makes it effortless.
slides.tip: Next step: write a generator function called squares(n) that yields 1, 4, 9 … n².
